notes.setup_flow_toolchain: Flow's actor-compiler builds and runs standalone (outside FoundationDB), and the real flow C++ runtime (Boost/OpenSSL/fmt/contrib deps) links and runs one real actor. Actor-compiler sub-step done (PR #1, Python port). Flow runtime linkage sub-step in progress (Boost umbrella-package fix pushed, result pending).
notes.vendor_picoquic_into_flow: SUPERSEDES the earlier wire_webtransportd_bridge milestone. Decision (2026-07-17): Flow terminates QUIC/HTTP-3/WebTransport directly, using flow's own IUDPSocket as the transport and a vendored picoquic+picotls+mbedtls stack (the same one webtransportd itself uses) linked into the Flow server process. One process, not two - no local IPC bridge needed at all. Deliberate, informed tradeoff: this puts untrusted-internet-facing QUIC/TLS parsing in the same process/address-space as the trusted game simulation (accepted, after being raised explicitly - see README). Not yet started.
notes.prove_http3_roundtrip: One real HTTP/3 message round-trips between a browser/client and the Flow server directly (Flow terminates QUIC itself - no webtransportd sidecar in this project's runtime path). No game state, no tick loop yet.
notes.add_tick_loop: A server-side loop ticks on an interval and holds one piece of state (e.g. a player's position). No input path yet.
notes.add_input_path: Client (Godot) input reaches the tick loop and updates that state.
notes.add_state_sync: Server pushes updated state back to the client, which renders it. This is the first genuine playing loop: one entity moving in one empty room.

generated_by: mcp__taskweft__plan against bootstrap-domain.json in this repo
revision_note: Major pivot same day: after the webtransportd-bridge local-IPC design (child_socket, cross-platform parity, handshake security) got too complicated, reconsidered whether the bridge was needed at all. Decided Flow terminates QUIC/HTTP-3 directly (vendoring picoquic+picotls+mbedtls), eliminating the bridge entirely, in exchange for losing the process-isolation security boundary between untrusted QUIC/TLS parsing and the trusted game simulation - an explicit, informed tradeoff, not an oversight. webtransportd itself remains a separate, independent project (its own PR #12, child_socket adapter, stays merged/available there) but is no longer part of this project's runtime architecture.
